era_context: The 11th century CE was a period of significant transformation across Eurasia. The Byzantine Empire was experiencing military pressure from the Seljuks and internal religious tensions. Western Europe was in the feudal period, with the Normans expanding in southern Italy and the Iberian Reconquista ongoing. The Song Dynasty in China was at its cultural and economic zenith. The Islamic world was fragmented among various dynasties and sultanates.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond major kingdoms (sparse documentation)

events.title: Harold Harefoot becomes king of England
events.description: Harold Harefoot (Harold I), the illegitimate son of Cnut the Great, became king of England in 1036 following his father's death in 1035. Harold's reign was marked by conflict with his half-brother Harthacnut over the division of the kingdom. Harold ruled England while Harthacnut ruled Denmark, but tensions between the two brothers would eventually lead to civil conflict. Harold's reign was brief (1036–1040) and marked by instability.
key_figures: Harold Harefoot (Harold I, r. 1036–1040, king of England)
events.certainty_note: Harold Harefoot's accession in 1036 is firmly attested in the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle and other contemporary sources.

events.title: Seljuk Turks establish control over Baghdad region
events.description: In 1036, the Seljuk Turks under Toğrul Bey were consolidating their control over the Baghdad region and surrounding territories. The Seljuks were gradually displacing the Buyid dynasty, which had ruled the region for over a century. By 1055, the Seljuks would formally take control of Baghdad and establish the Great Seljuk Empire. The year 1036 marks a significant step in this process of political consolidation.
events.certainty_note: Seljuk expansion toward Baghdad is well-attested, but the specific chronology of events in 1036 is less precise. The broader trend is confirmed, but individual campaigns may be misdated by ±5 years.

events.title: Norman mercenaries active in southern Italy
events.description: In 1036, Norman mercenaries and adventurers were actively engaged in military campaigns in southern Italy, serving various local lords and gradually establishing themselves as a major military force. The Normans were displacing Byzantine and Lombard authorities and beginning the process that would lead to the conquest of Sicily and the establishment of the Norman Kingdom of the Two Sicilies.

sources.name: William of Apulia, Gesta Roberti Wiscardi
Geoffrey Malaterra, De rebus gestis Rogerii Calabriae et Siciliae comitis

events.certainty_note: Norman expansion in southern Italy is well-attested, though specific dates for individual campaigns in 1036 are not always precisely documented. The broader trend is confirmed.

events.title: Byzantine Empire under Constantine VIII and Zoe Porphyrogenneta
events.description: In 1036, the Byzantine Empire was ruled by Constantine VIII (r. 1025–1028) and his sister Zoe Porphyrogenneta (r. 1028–1050). Constantine VIII died in 1028, so by 1036, Zoe was the reigning empress, though she was aging and increasingly dependent on advisors. The year 1036 falls within a period of relative stability, though the empire was beginning to face military pressure from the Seljuks and other external threats.
events.certainty_note: Zoe Porphyrogenneta's reign dates are firmly established in Byzantine sources.

events.title: Song Dynasty continues under Emperor Renzong
events.description: In 1036, the Song Dynasty under Emperor Renzong (r. 1022–1063) was experiencing continued prosperity and cultural achievement. The Song court was a center of artistic, literary, and philosophical innovation, with advances in printing, ceramics, and landscape painting. The year 1036 falls within a period of relative peace and stability that allowed cultural patronage and technological innovation to flourish.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Song Dynasty records are extensive and well-preserved. Emperor Renzong's reign is firmly documented.

disconfirming_evidence: Constantine VIII died in 1028, not 1036, so the reference to him as reigning in 1036 is incorrect. Zoe Porphyrogenneta was the reigning empress in 1036. Toğrul Bey's reign is traditionally dated to 1038–1063, so his prominence in 1036 is slightly anachronistic; he was rising to power but had not yet formally established his rule.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1036 is moderate, with strong records from the Byzantine Empire, Song China, and Western European kingdoms. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward court chronicles and ecclesiastical records. Scandinavian and Central Asian sources are sparse and often unreliable for this period. The historical record is biased toward political and military events, with less attention to economic, social, and technological developments. The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle provides detailed information about English events, but sources for southern Italy and the Seljuk world are less contemporary and more subject to later interpretation.

graph_edges.note: Harold Harefoot's accession was caused by the death of Cnut the Great in 1035.
Seljuk consolidation in 1036 led to the formal takeover of Baghdad in 1055.
Norman expansion in southern Italy in 1036 was part of the broader expansion that led to the conquest of Sicily.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
